key_insight: A lone-pair-rich hybrid perovskite can be engineered to make the lattice unusually soft, suppressing phonon heat flow to achieve ultralow thermal conductivity.
engineering_relevance: This offers a chemistry-first route to low-thermal-conductivity materials for thermoelectric cooling and thermal insulation without relying only on nanoscale structuring.
method: Physics-guided symbolic regression and machine learning to identify a lone-pair-dominated composition space.
Mechanochemical synthesis of the selected candidate, GuaPbI3.
Thermal, electrical, impedance, and DFT analyses to link structure, phonons, and charge transport.
evidence_signal: The synthesized GuaPbI3 showed room-temperature thermal conductivity of 0.088 W m^-1 K^-1, with impedance and electrical measurements indicating bulk-dominated, bias-dependent conduction.
watch_item: The electronically accessible transport appears sensitive to bias and chemical potential, so device performance may be highly morphology- and operating-condition-dependent.
paper_title: Lone-Pair-Induced Lattice Softness Enables Ultralow Thermal Conductivity in Hybrid Organic-Inorganic Perovskite GuaPbI$_3$
